disease.name: Clostridial Sepsis with Massive Intravascular Hemolysis
disease.note: C. perfringens (most common), C. septicum causing massive intravascular hemolysis from alpha-toxin (phospholipase C/lecithinase). Classic: post-septic abortion, biliary surgery/ERCP, trauma, GI malignancy. Dramatic presentation: massive hemoglobinuria (port-wine urine), rapidly deepening jaundice, shock, renal failure.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Clostridial sepsis with hemolysis -> EMERGENCY ICU; Gas gangrene -> EMERGENCY surgery; Any suspicion -> IMMEDIATE antibiotics and resuscitation

examFeatures.note: Rapidly developing within hours, bronze skin discoloration

firstLine.note: Hemolytic anemia, spherocytes, neutrophilia with left shift
Predominantly indirect from hemolysis; rapidly rising
Look for gas in tissues, abscess
Acute kidney injury from hemoglobin nephrotoxicity

emergency.action: IV metronidazole 500 mg q8h + IV ceftriaxone 2g q12h or penicillin G 4 million U q4h
Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (NS or LR, 30 mL/kg bolus)
Vasopressors (norepinephrine) for septic shock refractory to fluids
Emergent surgical consultation for debridement if gas gangrene or abscess
Exchange transfusion if severe massive hemolysis not responding

inpatient.action: Continue IV antibiotics for 14-21 days (metronidazole + ceftriaxone/penicillin)
Renal replacement therapy (hemodialysis) for acute kidney injury
Transfusion support: PRBCs as needed, platelets if DIC with bleeding
Monitor LDH, haptoglobin, bilirubin, hemoglobin daily to track hemolysis resolution

refractory.action: Exchange transfusion or plasmapheresis for refractory massive hemolysis
Consider ECMO for refractory septic shock

preventive.action: Appropriate antibiotic prophylaxis for GI/biliary surgery
Timely surgical intervention for intra-abdominal infections
Good wound care for traumatic injuries

disease.pathophysiology: Clostridium perfringens produces alpha-toxin (lecithinase/phospholipase C), a zinc metalloenzyme that enzymatically cleaves phospholipids (phosphatidylcholine, sphingomyelin) in red blood cell membranes. This causes rapid osmotic lysis of RBCs leading to massive intravascular hemolysis. Free hemoglobin released overwhelms haptoglobin binding capacity, causing hemoglobinuria (port-wine urine) and acute kidney injury from hemoglobin cast nephropathy and renal vasoconstriction. Alpha-toxin also causes platelet aggregation, endothelial injury, and activation of coagulation cascade leading to DIC. Other toxins (theta-toxin/perfringolysin O) contribute to cardiotoxicity and hepatotoxicity. Sepsis-induced cholestasis further elevates bilirubin. In gas gangrene, organism produces gas in tissues (CO2 and H2 from fermentation) causing crepitus.

diagnosticClues.clue: Rapidly deepening jaundice within hours in septic patient
Massively elevated LDH with undetectable haptoglobin
History of recent GI/biliary surgery or septic abortion
Gas in tissues on imaging or crepitus on exam

exclusionClues.clue: Alternative cause of hemolysis confirmed (transfusion reaction, autoimmune, malaria)
No evidence of gas on imaging and no crepitus
